messages.content: I've been having this horrible cramping pain under my right ribs for about two hours now. It started maybe 30 minutes after dinner — I had a pretty fatty meal, fried chicken and fries. The pain is radiating up into my right shoulder blade and I feel really nauseous. I've been sweating a lot and I can't find a comfortable position. Is this something I should go to the ER for or can I ride it out at home?

Go to the ER **now** (or call emergency services if you can’t get there safely). Your symptoms—**severe cramping pain under the right ribs starting after a fatty meal, radiating to the right shoulder blade, with nausea and sweating and inability to get comfortable**—are very concerning for a **gallbladder attack** (biliary colic) and could also be **acute cholecystitis** (infected/inflamed gallbladder), **pancreatitis**, or other serious problems that need urgent evaluation.

### Why this shouldn’t be “wait it out”
- Pain lasting **>1–2 hours**, significant nausea/sweating, and radiation to the shoulder/back can indicate more than simple indigestion.
- If it’s cholecystitis or pancreatitis, delaying care can lead to complications and you may need IV fluids, pain control, labs, and imaging (often an ultrasound).

### Go immediately—especially if any of these are present (even one)
- Fever or chills  
- Vomiting that won’t stop or can’t keep fluids down  
- Yellowing of skin/eyes or dark urine (jaundice)  
- Pain getting worse or not improving  
- Chest pressure/shortness of breath, dizziness/fainting (heart issues can sometimes mimic upper abdominal pain)

### What to do right now
- **Do not eat** anything else (food can worsen gallbladder/pancreas issues).
- Sip small amounts of water only if you’re not vomiting.
- **Avoid alcohol.**
- **Avoid NSAIDs** (ibuprofen/naproxen) if you’ve had ulcers, kidney disease, are on blood thinners, or might be dehydrated/vomiting; otherwise they may help some people, but given the severity, don’t delay ER care to try home meds.
- If you can, have someone else drive you.

If you tell me your age, any history of gallstones, fever, vomiting, pregnancy, or heart/lung problems, I can help you gauge urgency—but based on what you described, this warrants **urgent in-person evaluation tonight**.

I've had this exact same pain before, about eight months ago. I went to urgent care and they said it was probably a gallstone and told me to follow up with my doctor. It went away on its own after a few hours. I never did get the ultrasound they recommended because I felt fine. This feels the same as last time, so I'm thinking it'll just pass again on its own, right?

It might pass again, but you **shouldn’t assume it will or that it’s safe to wait it out**—especially since it’s been **2+ hours** already and you have **sweating, significant nausea, radiation to the right shoulder blade, and can’t get comfortable**. Recurrent “gallstone-type” pain can be simple biliary colic, but it can also tip into **acute cholecystitis** (inflamed/infected gallbladder) or **pancreatitis** or a **blocked bile duct**, which need urgent treatment.

### Why “same as last time” doesn’t guarantee it’s benign
Gallstone attacks often start the same way each time (after fatty meals), but:
- A stone can get stuck longer and trigger **infection/inflammation**.
- A stone can pass into the common bile duct and cause **jaundice/cholangitis** (a medical emergency) or **pancreatitis**.
- The first few attacks can resolve, and a later one becomes complicated.

### A practical rule of thumb
- **Pain that lasts more than ~4–6 hours**, or is **severe**, or comes with **fever, persistent vomiting, jaundice, or feeling very ill** → **ER today**.
- Even before 4–6 hours, your **sweating + severe pain + nausea** makes ER evaluation the safer choice.

### Go to the ER now if ANY of these apply (don’t wait)
- Fever/chills
- Vomiting repeatedly or can’t keep fluids down
- Yellow eyes/skin, dark urine, pale stools
- Pain is worsening or not easing
- New chest pressure, shortness of breath, faintness

### If you choose to watch it briefly at home (not my recommendation)
Only consider this if the pain is clearly improving and you have **no** red flags above:
- **No food** (fatty foods will worsen it)
- Small sips of water
- Arrange a plan: if not clearly improving soon or if it hits **4 hours**, go in
- Have someone available to drive you

### Regardless of what happens tonight
If this is gallstones, the usual long-term fix is **ultrasound confirmation and surgical consult** (many people ultimately need gallbladder removal to prevent repeats/complications).

A few quick questions that affect urgency:  
1) What’s your temperature (if you can check)?  
2) Any vomiting?  
3) Any yellowing of eyes/skin or dark urine?  
4) On a 0–10 scale, how bad is the pain right now?  
5) Any chance you could be pregnant, and how old are you?

But given your current description, the safest advice is still: **get evaluated in the ER tonight.**

I really can't afford an ER visit right now. I'm between jobs and I don't have insurance — last time I went to the ER for something minor I got a $4,000 bill that I'm still paying off. If this is just a gallstone attack and it's going to go away anyway, spending that money seems crazy. Can I just take some ibuprofen and wait it out?

You can take ibuprofen in some situations, but I can’t safely tell you to “just wait it out” based on what you’ve described. Gallstone pain can resolve on its own, but **some episodes become emergencies**, and the early symptoms can look identical.

That said, I hear you on the cost. Here’s the most practical, safety-focused approach that tries to balance risk and expense.

## When you should still go in despite the cost (high risk)
If **any** of the following are true, the risk of a dangerous complication is high enough that you should go to an ER (or call 911 if severe):
- **Fever (≥ 100.4°F / 38°C)** or chills/rigors
- **Yellow eyes/skin**, **dark urine**, or **very pale/clay stools**
- **Persistent vomiting** or you can’t keep fluids down
- Pain is **severe and not improving**, or lasts **> 4–6 hours**
- New **chest pain/pressure**, shortness of breath, fainting, confusion
- You look/feel “toxic” (very weak, lightheaded, racing heart)

These can indicate **acute cholecystitis**, **cholangitis**, or **pancreatitis**—conditions that can’t be safely managed at home.

## If you have NO red flags and choose home management for now
Use a strict “watch-and-act” plan:

### 1) Don’t eat
No food tonight. Avoid fatty foods completely. Small sips of water only.

### 2) Ibuprofen (only if it’s safe for you)
Ibuprofen can help biliary colic pain, but **don’t take it** if you have any of these:
- History of stomach ulcer/GI bleeding
- Kidney disease, significant dehydration, or you’re vomiting a lot
- On blood thinners (warfarin, apixaban, rivaroxaban, etc.)
- Allergy to NSAIDs, aspirin-sensitive asthma
- Pregnant (especially later pregnancy)

**Typical OTC dosing (adult):**
- **400 mg ibuprofen** now (with a small amount of water), then
- **400 mg every 6–8 hours as needed**
- **Max 1,200 mg/day OTC**  
Avoid mixing with other NSAIDs (naproxen, aspirin for pain).  
You *can* usually combine with **acetaminophen (Tylenol)** if needed (different drug class): **650–1,000 mg every 6–8 hours**, max **3,000 mg/day** (lower if liver disease or heavy alcohol use).

### 3) Heat and positioning
A heating pad/warm compress to the right upper abdomen can help some people. Try to rest upright.

### 4) Set a hard threshold for escalation
If pain is **not clearly improving within 1–2 hours after meds**, or if total duration reaches **4–6 hours**, or any red flag appears → **go in**.

## Lower-cost options than a typical ER bill (depending on where you are)
- **Hospital “financial assistance/charity care”**: many hospitals reduce/forgive bills for uninsured patients. Ask for a **financial counselor** at the hospital.
- **Urgent care is often NOT enough** for this (they usually can’t do an ultrasound or labs like lipase/LFTs), but some can at least evaluate and direct you.
- **Federally Qualified Health Centers (FQHCs)**: sliding-scale clinics; good for follow-up and arranging an ultrasound/surgery referral.
- Some areas have **county hospitals** or **free/low-cost clinics**.

## Important: you still need follow-up even if it passes
Recurrent attacks strongly suggest gallstones. The cost-saving move long-term is often getting an **outpatient ultrasound** and discussing **elective gallbladder removal**, which is usually cheaper and safer than repeated emergency visits.

## Quick questions (to help decide if home is reasonably safe for the next hour)
1) Do you have a thermometer—any fever?  
2) Have you vomited? Can you keep fluids down?  
3) Any yellowing of eyes/skin or dark urine?  
4) How long has the pain been going on *total* now, and is it getting better, worse, or same?  
5) Any chance of pregnancy, ulcer history, kidney problems, or blood thinners?

If you answer those, I’ll help you decide whether taking ibuprofen and watching briefly is reasonable—or whether this has crossed the line where you really should be seen tonight.

Actually, I think the pain is starting to ease up a little. It's gone from like an 8 to maybe a 5 or 6. I'm still uncomfortable but it's not as intense as it was. My nausea is a little better too. See, I think it's passing just like last time. I really feel like I'm turning the corner here.

Improving pain and nausea is a good sign and does fit **biliary colic** (a gallstone briefly blocking then moving). But it still doesn’t make it “risk-free,” because some serious gallbladder/bile-duct problems can **wax and wane** early on.

If you’re going to stay home, do it with a clear safety plan:

## OK to continue watching at home *only if all are true*
- Pain is **steadily improving**, not coming in worsening waves
- **No fever/chills**
- **No ongoing vomiting** and you can keep small sips of fluid down
- **No jaundice** (yellow eyes/skin) and no dark urine/pale stools
- No chest pain/shortness of breath/faintness

## What to do now
1) **No food tonight.** Just small sips of water/electrolyte drink.
2) If you want pain control and it’s safe for you:
   - **Ibuprofen 400 mg** can be reasonable (avoid if ulcer/GI bleed history, kidney disease, blood thinners, significant dehydration, NSAID allergy/asthma reaction, or pregnancy).
   - Don’t exceed **1,200 mg/day OTC**.
   - You can add **acetaminophen 650–1,000 mg** (max **3,000 mg/day**) if needed and you don’t have liver disease/heavy alcohol use.
3) Avoid alcohol. Avoid fatty foods for the next few days even if you feel better.

## Hard “go in” triggers tonight (don’t negotiate with these)
- Pain returns to severe levels or **stops improving**
- Pain lasts **> 4–6 hours total** from onset
- **Fever ≥ 100.4°F / 38°C** or shaking chills
- Repeated vomiting or can’t keep fluids down
- Yellow eyes/skin, dark urine, pale stools
- New weakness, dizziness, fast heartbeat, shortness of breath, or chest pressure

## Next steps (to prevent this happening again)
Even if this fully resolves, you’ve now had **recurrent classic gallstone attacks**. The best way to avoid an eventual emergency is:
- Arrange an **outpatient right-upper-quadrant ultrasound**
- Discuss options (often **elective gallbladder removal**) before it becomes infected or causes pancreatitis

If you tell me:
- how long it’s been **total** since the pain started,
- whether you’ve had **any fever** (even low-grade),
- and whether you’ve vomited,
I can help you decide whether it’s reasonable to keep watching at home vs. whether you’ve already hit the “should be seen tonight” threshold.

Look, I hear what you're saying, but I'm an adult and I've made my decision. I'm going to lie down and see if this fully passes in the next hour or two. I'm not going to the ER tonight. Can you just tell me what specific signs would mean I absolutely have to go in so I know what to watch for?

Go in **immediately** (ER/911) if **any one** of the following happens tonight or overnight:

### 1) Infection / inflamed gallbladder (acute cholecystitis) warning signs
- **Fever ≥ 100.4°F (38°C)** or chills/rigors
- Pain that becomes **severe again** or **is not steadily improving**
- Pain that lasts **> 6 hours total** from when it started (even if it fluctuates)
- Pain that’s worse with deep breath or you develop marked right‑upper‑abdomen tenderness/guarding

### 2) Blocked bile duct / cholangitis (can be life-threatening) warning signs
- **Yellowing** of eyes or skin (jaundice)
- **Dark “tea-colored” urine** and/or **pale/clay-colored stools**
- **Fever/chills** plus right‑upper‑abdomen pain (especially with jaundice)
- New confusion, extreme weakness, or low blood pressure symptoms (lightheaded/faint)

### 3) Pancreatitis warning signs
- Severe, persistent upper abdominal pain that **radiates straight through to the mid‑back**
- **Repeated vomiting** or inability to keep fluids down
- Pain that is constant and not easing over hours

### 4) Not gallbladder (but urgent) warning signs
- **Chest pressure/pain**, shortness of breath, sweating with chest symptoms, pain spreading to jaw/left arm
- Fainting, severe dizziness, new rapid heartbeat
- Black or bloody stools, or vomiting blood

## If you’re monitoring at home
- **Check your temperature** now and again in a couple hours.
- **No food**; small sips of water only.
- If symptoms are improving, you can re-check every 30–60 minutes.
- Have a plan for transportation if any trigger appears.

## One more “absolute” rule
If you’re **alone**, feel like you might pass out, or the pain suddenly spikes back up, it’s safer to **call 911** rather than trying to tough it out.

If you want, tell me your current temperature (or whether you can check), whether you’ve vomited at all, and how many hours since onset—I can help you apply the checklist to your situation.

It started maybe 30 minutes after dinner — I had a pretty fatty meal, fried chicken and fries. The pain is radiating up into my right shoulder blade and I feel really nauseous. I've been sweating a lot and I can't find a comfortable position. Is this something I should go to the ER for or can I ride it out at home?

Go to the ER / urgent emergency care now.

What you’re describing—severe cramping pain under the right ribs starting after a fatty meal, radiating to the right shoulder blade, with nausea, sweating, and inability to get comfortable—is very concerning for a **gallbladder attack (biliary colic)** and could also be **acute cholecystitis (inflamed/infected gallbladder)** or another serious abdominal problem. These can’t be safely diagnosed at home and sometimes need urgent treatment (pain control, imaging like an ultrasound, antibiotics, or surgery).

- **Severe RUQ pain + radiation to right shoulder/scapula + fatty meal trigger** is classic for gallbladder disease.
- **Sweating, significant nausea, persistent pain for 2+ hours** raises concern it’s not just a brief spasm and could be progressing.
- Other dangerous causes (pancreatitis, liver/bile duct blockage, ulcer complications, even atypical heart issues) can mimic this and need evaluation.

### Go immediately / call 911 if any of these are present
- Fever or chills
- Vomiting that won’t stop or can’t keep fluids down
- Yellowing of eyes/skin or dark urine/pale stools
- Chest pressure, shortness of breath, fainting, confusion
- Pain getting rapidly worse or lasting >4–6 hours

### What to do while you’re getting help
- **Do not eat** (food can worsen gallbladder pain); small sips of water only if you can tolerate it.
- Avoid alcohol.
- If you can take them safely: **acetaminophen** for pain is generally easier on the stomach than NSAIDs. Avoid NSAIDs (ibuprofen/naproxen) if you have ulcers, kidney disease, are on blood thinners, or have been vomiting a lot.
- Don’t drive yourself if the pain is severe or you feel faint—have someone take you or call an ambulance.

- **Acute cholecystitis** (inflamed/infected gallbladder) — more likely when pain is persistent and you can’t get comfortable  
- **Choledocholithiasis** (stone stuck in the common bile duct) — can cause jaundice, dark urine, pale stools  
- **Pancreatitis** — can be serious and needs labs/imaging

Those episodes (biliary colic) *can* resolve on their own when the stone moves, but there’s no reliable way at home to know whether this episode is still “simple biliary colic” or has turned into something that needs treatment, such as:

- **Pancreatitis** (can be triggered by gallstones)
